scenarios.title: Delta — add waitlists to the course platform

top_gaps: Role-based authorization matrix: roles (owner/admin/agent) and 'by Agent/Admin' triggers are named, but no UC/AC specifies the permission rules the agent must enforce.

Status-transition state machine: legal transitions and the 'immutable once shipped/declined' rule are asserted in ACs but neither modeled (no CHECK/trigger) nor specified as a transition table — the agent must invent enforcement.
Org-scoped FK validation: ACs require member/release to belong to the same organization, but the model only enforces global existence, so cross-tenant assignment/attachment would silently succeed.
Auto-creation of status-history entries: the 'whenever status changes, record an entry' policy is stated but its implementation (DB trigger vs app code) is undefined.
End-user submission surface: the embedded-form ingestion path (org identification, email validation, abuse handling) is acknowledged in the clarifications log but never specified.

Status-transition enforcement: DAs expect a 'check' error and the plan promises a state machine, but model.dbml has no CHECK/trigger defining valid or terminal (shipped/declined) transitions — agent must invent it.
Auto status-history creation mechanism (DB trigger vs application code) is asserted as a policy but never specified.
End-user feedback ingestion: no public submission endpoint/contract and no spec for how the embedded form identifies and authorizes the target organization.
Authorization model: owner/admin/agent roles exist but per-use-case permission enforcement is unspecified.
'Full audit trail' (brief) vs reality: only status changes are logged; assignments, comments, and release changes have no audit home.

How a course is determined to be 'full' and how over-enrollment is prevented — the trigger for the entire waitlist feature, but no UC, constraint, or seat-count is specified (UC-001 enrolls with no capacity check).
Notifications referenced in FL-005/FL-006 ('Student notified of offer') — never specified as a UC or integration, nor placed out of scope.
Whether waitlist ordering is authoritative on queue_position or created_at — both exist and UC-005 references queue position while indexes/seed lean on created_at.

Which artifact is authoritative when model.dbml and the generated schema.sql disagree (missing waitlists.queue_position and enrollments.updated_at, name vs full_name, CASCADE vs RESTRICT on enrollments.course_id).
Role/authorization enforcement: distinct Owner/Admin/Agent powers are specified in prose but no UC, data assertion, or task operationalizes them — the agent must invent the entire authz layer.
Same-org integrity between feedback_items and its assignee/release/end_user references is not enforced (FKs point to global PKs), contradicting the stated no-cross-org-leakage NFR.
Status lifecycle is described as ordered but UC-008 permits any enum-to-any-enum transition; the agent must decide whether to constrain it.
Atomicity/consistency of the status update plus its history entry (and feedback_items.status mirroring the latest history row) is unspecified.
Authentication & password storage — UC-001 collects a password but the schema models no credentials and no auth flow exists
Booking state-machine guards — legal transitions are unenforced, and 'rate only a completed booking' is prose-only (schema enforces just one-rating-per-booking)
Authorization/ownership rules — only-the-targeted-provider-accepts and only-the-customer-rates are implied by actor labels but never made checkable
Payment lifecycle — who triggers 'refunded' and the legal payment_status transitions are unspecified

Define 'enrolled-count' in the courses capacity trigger — it must count only status='enrolled' (excluding dropped/waitlisted), otherwise a freed seat is never seen and UC-007 accept-enrollment is blocked by the capacity check.

Specify where the seat-offer policy lives (DB trigger vs. application service): FL-003/FL-006 and UC-006/UC-008-AC-2 describe 'offer the earliest waiting entry on drop/decline' but the data model only encodes the offered status, not what fires the transition.
Clarify or drop FL-001's 'Recount course seats' policy/event — it has no use case, data assertion, or distinct effect, so a builder must guess whether it's a no-op or a real responsibility.

The role permission matrix: which role may perform each action and how an unauthorized action is rejected — promised in the brief ('pinned in ddd-usecases') but never turned into testable acceptance criteria.

Cross-tenant write enforcement: ACs assert 'same organization' for assignee/release links, but neither composite FKs nor any live test enforce it — the agent must invent org-scoping guards on every write.
Append-only enforcement mechanism for status_changes (DB-level trigger/revoke vs. application convention) — the requirement is first-class but its enforcement point is unstated.
Authentication/session handling for members (out of scope per brief, but required to actually ship the app).
How a booking's price is set at acceptance — provider-entered or computed from hourly_rate x (ends_at - starts_at)? UC-003 only says 'agreed price', leaving the agent to guess the pricing rule.

The entire application/API layer is unspecified (endpoints, payloads, framework) and auth + per-account data isolation are deferred — the agent must invent the enforcement mechanism behind the UC-009/010 isolation tests.
TypeID generation (prefix scheme is documented, but the library/mechanism to mint acct_/svc_/book_/rating_ ids is not pinned).
Capacity / over-enrollment is modeled (courses.capacity) but no use case, AC, or data assertion enforces it — the brief's stated reason for the system (double bookings) is left unspecified.
The `completed` status and FL-002 (Complete enrollment) have no use case and no plan task; the builder must invent the completion flow end-to-end.
FL-001's 'Recount course seats' policy has no use case or task — unclear if seat counts are derived, cached, or ignored.

Re-enrollment behavior under the unique (student_id, course_id) constraint is undefined: a dropped enrollment leaves a row, so re-enrolling the same student in the same course would collide, and nothing says what should happen.
